subject: Chemistry
title: Antivirals: From HIV to COVID
description: A micro-lesson that makes Antivirals: From HIV to COVID usable.

hook.text: HIV was a death sentence in 1985. By 2000, antiviral cocktails turned it into a manageable condition. Chemistry saved millions.

buildup.text: Viruses hijack host cell machinery to reproduce. Antivirals target specific steps in the viral replication cycle.

discovery.text: AZT blocks reverse transcriptase—the enzyme HIV uses to copy its RNA into DNA. Without it, the virus can't reproduce.

twist.text: Using one drug breeds resistance. HAART therapy combines 3+ drugs targeting different steps, making resistance nearly impossible.

climax.text: Paxlovid blocks SARS-CoV-2's protease enzyme. Built on HIV antiviral principles, it was approved for COVID in just 22 months.

punchline.text: Target the enzyme, block the cycle, stop the virus. Antiviral chemistry saves lives.

quiz.question: Why does HIV treatment require multiple drugs simultaneously?
options: Using one drug alone allows the virus to develop resistance; multiple drugs prevent this
Each drug only works in a specific organ of the body
HIV exists in three forms that each require a different drug
